refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed absolute.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure across multiple teleoperation sessions and load profiles. Acceptance: sustained packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) over a minimum test duration (e.g., 1 hour continuous operation).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall monitor network link quality and alert operators when packet loss exceeds threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The NGHE solution shall provide configurable packet loss acceptance criteria aligned with customer network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission in the teleoperation protocol stack, with real-time packet loss monitoring and dynamic codec adjustment.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover and load balancing to reduce single-point packet loss.
DP-C: Implement application-level acknowledgment and timeout-driven retransmission with prioritization of safety-critical commands over telemetry.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (FEC/retransmission) with DP-C (application-level ACK) and integrate packet loss metrics into SafetyAlert feedback loop.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layer packet loss mitigation strategy combining (1) protocol-level forward error correction and selective retransmission, (2) application-level command acknowledgment and timeout-driven retry with safety-critical prioritization, and (3) real-time packet loss monitoring integrated into the SafetyAlert interface to notify operators and safety systems when thresholds are approached or exceeded.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol design (FEC/retransmission layer) with application logic (command ACK/retry) and safety alerting (SafetyAlert integration). This is acceptable because: (1) packet loss mitigation is inherently a cross-layer concern in real-time teleoperation, (2) safety criticality justifies tight integration of monitoring with alert generation, (3) the coupling is localized to the Teleoperation module boundary.
coupling_check.notes: Decoupling DP-D into separate protocol-only and application-only solutions would weaken packet loss resilience and delay safety alerts. The selected DP accepts necessary coupling in service of safety and performance requirements.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment. Risk: If actual network conditions differ significantly, packet loss targets may not be achievable without additional infrastructure investment.

Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth and latency) is acceptable within teleoperation bandwidth budget. Risk: FEC may increase latency or consume bandwidth needed for high-fidelity video/sensor feedback.

Assumption: SafetyAlert interface can accommodate real-time packet loss metrics without introducing additional latency. Risk: Alert generation latency could delay operator awareness of degraded communication.
Assumption: Operator training and procedures account for packet loss scenarios and alert responses. Risk: Operators may not respond appropriately to packet loss alerts, reducing safety benefit.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC algorithm selection (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget, (2) retransmission timeout and retry limits, (3) packet loss threshold for SafetyAlert trigger, (4) prioritization rules for safety-critical vs. telemetry packets. These details should be deferred to sub-level decomposition but should be noted as critical design decisions.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including protocol-level packet loss mitigation (FEC, retransmission), application-level command acknowledgment and retry logic, packet loss monitoring, and integration with SafetyAlert for real-time operator notification. The module interfaces with Vehicle (for command execution and telemetry collection), Safety Services (for alert generation and safety policy enforcement), and the Network Link (for physical transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and tasking requests from tele-operator. Teleoperation module applies packet loss mitigation and acknowledgment logic to ensure command delivery.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and operational feedback to tele-operator. Teleoperation module monitors packet loss on telemetry stream and reports metrics to SafetyAlert.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends packet loss status and alerts to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds threshold. Enables safety system to enforce operational constraints or alert operator.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical communication channel (e.g., cellular, satellite, WiFi). Teleoperation module applies FEC and retransmission over this link and monitors link quality metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary. Packet loss mitigation is a specific concern of the Teleoperation subsystem, not a system-wide concern. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns physical equipment, actuation, and local sensors. Packet loss mitigation is a communication concern, not a vehicle hardware concern. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple communication logic with vehicle control, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of teleoperation communication quality. It directly owns the protocol stack, command/telemetry processing, and operator interface. Packet loss mitigation is a core teleoperation concern. Highly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alert generation. While SafetyAlert is generated in response to packet loss, the mitigation logic itself (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) is not a safety service concern. Assigning IM to Safety Services would blur the boundary between communication quality and safety policy. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary because: (1) it owns the teleoperation protocol stack and communication logic, (2) packet loss mitigation is intrinsic to teleoperation quality-of-service, (3) it directly interfaces with Network Link and can monitor link quality, (4) it generates Telemetry and Tasking flows that are subject to packet loss, (5) it can integrate packet loss monitoring with SafetyAlert without diffusion. Ownership is clear and localized.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it owns both protocol-level (FEC, retransmission) and application-level (command ACK, retry) logic. Mitigation: Decompose into sub-modules (e.g., Protocol Handler, Command Manager) at next level.

Risk: Packet loss monitoring and SafetyAlert integration may introduce latency if not carefully designed. Mitigation: Specify real-time constraints and alert generation latency budget in sub-level DP.

Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient visibility into packet loss metrics (e.g., if Network Link is a black-box third-party service). Mitigation: Establish interface contract with Network Link to expose packet loss statistics.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has access to Network Link packet loss metrics (e.g., via a monitoring interface or API). If Network Link does not expose these metrics, escalate to system level to establish a monitoring interface. Additionally, define the SafetyAlert contract: specify packet loss threshold, alert frequency, and alert payload format to ensure Safety Services can act on the information.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layer packet loss mitigation) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (maintain packet loss ≤1%). The DP specifies concrete mechanisms (FEC, retransmission, monitoring, SafetyAlert integration) that, if implemented correctly, will achieve the acceptance criterion. The DP is traceable to the FR and does not introduce extraneous functionality.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If Teleoperation module is not given clear ownership of Network Link interface, packet loss monitoring may be diffused across multiple modules (e.g., Vehicle, Safety Services). Mitigation: Establish explicit interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link.

Diffusion Risk 2: If SafetyAlert integration is not carefully scoped, Teleoperation may become tightly coupled to Safety Services policy logic. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as a unidirectional notification interface; Safety Services owns policy enforcement, not Teleoperation.

Diffusion Risk 3: If FEC and retransmission logic is not encapsulated within Teleoperation, it may leak into Vehicle or Safety Services modules. Mitigation: Establish clear module boundaries and enforce encapsulation at architectural review.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of DP-D. All packet loss mitigation logic (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) is contained within Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not require diffusion of responsibility. Containment is strong.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics (via Telemetry or SafetyAlert) for measurement. Test harness can inject packet loss and verify mitigation effectiveness. Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. The module owns all mechanisms required to achieve the acceptance criterion. No ambiguity regarding responsibility or interface ownership.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Establish explicit interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link to specify packet loss metric exposure (e.g., API, monitoring port, log format).
Revision 2: Define SafetyAlert payload schema for packet loss notifications, including threshold, current packet loss %, alert severity, and recommended operator action.
Revision 3: Specify FEC algorithm, retransmission timeout, and retry limits in sub-level DP decomposition. These are critical design decisions that should not be deferred.
Revision 4: Establish latency budget for packet loss detection and SafetyAlert generation to ensure operator awareness is timely.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, network monitoring, configurable criteria) that must be individually addressed at the next decomposition level. DP-D is sound and well-justified, but its implementation requires detailed design decisions (FEC algorithm, timeout values, alert thresholds) that belong at the sub-level. Teleoperation module is the correct IM boundary, but its internal architecture (sub-modules for protocol handling, command management, monitoring) should be defined at the next level. Proceeding to sub-level decomposition is necessary to achieve sufficient design detail for implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting (measure and expose packet loss metrics to monitoring and safety systems).
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact (implement FEC and/or selective retransmission).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall monitor network link quality and alert operators when packet loss exceeds threshold (integrate with SafetyAlert and operator interface).
FR-NGHE-009.4: The NGHE solution shall provide configurable packet loss acceptance criteria aligned with customer network conditions (support customer-defined thresholds and network profiles).

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment.
FEC overhead (bandwidth and latency) is acceptable within teleoperation bandwidth budget.
SafetyAlert interface can accommodate real-time packet loss metrics without introducing unacceptable latency.
Operator training and procedures account for packet loss scenarios and alert responses.
Network Link interface exposes packet loss metrics (e.g., via API or monitoring port).
Safety Services owns policy enforcement; Teleoperation owns communication quality.
open_risks: Risk: Actual network conditions may differ from customer-agreed conditions, making packet loss targets unachievable without additional infrastructure.
Risk: FEC overhead may increase latency or consume bandwidth needed for high-fidelity feedback.
Risk: Operator may not respond appropriately to packet loss alerts, reducing safety benefit.
Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it owns both protocol-level and application-level logic; requires careful sub-module decomposition.
Risk: SafetyAlert integration may introduce latency if not carefully designed; requires real-time constraint specification.
evidence_needed: Network condition profile from customer (latency, jitter, bandwidth, packet loss baseline).
Teleoperation bandwidth budget and latency constraints.
SafetyAlert interface specification and latency budget.
Operator training and procedure documentation for packet loss scenarios.
Network Link interface specification (packet loss metric exposure).
FEC algorithm selection and overhead analysis (bandwidth, latency, computational cost).
Retransmission timeout and retry limit analysis (trade-off between latency and reliability).
Packet loss threshold definition and SafetyAlert trigger criteria.
End-to-end packet loss measurement test plan and acceptance criteria.
